heroOverview: Ancylostomiasis is infection with the hookworm Ancylostoma duodenale, a blood-sucking nematode that penetrates bare skin, migrates to the small intestine, and attaches to the mucosal wall to feed on human blood — consuming an estimated 0.2 mL of blood per worm per day. It is the cause of one half of the global hookworm burden, with A. duodenale predominant in the Mediterranean basin, Middle East, India, and parts of East Asia; Necator americanus accounts for most cases in the Americas and sub-Saharan Africa. Together, soil-transmitted hookworms infect approximately 472 million people globally according to WHO estimates, making them among the most prevalent parasitic infections worldwide. The defining clinical consequence is chronic iron-deficiency anemia and protein malnutrition, which disproportionately affect children, pregnant women, and agricultural workers. Anthelminthic treatment with benzimidazoles is safe, inexpensive, and highly effective.

keyStats.prevalence: Ancylostoma duodenale and Necator americanus together infect approximately 472 million people worldwide (WHO, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly affects children aged 1–14 and adults in agricultural work in tropical and subtropical low-income countries
keyStats.avgAge: Heaviest worm burdens occur in school-age children 5–14 years; adults are also heavily affected in endemic agricultural settings
keyStats.globalCases: Soil-transmitted helminths as a group are responsible for 1.5–5.1 million disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) annually, with hookworm contributing the majority

definition: Ancylostomiasis (ICD-10: B76.0) is intestinal infection caused by Ancylostoma duodenale, a blood-sucking nematode of the family Ancylostomatidae. The species name refers to its characteristic hooked anterior end, which bears cutting plates allowing firm attachment to intestinal mucosa. Adult worms measure 8–13 mm and live for 1–5 years in the proximal small intestine, where each female produces 10,000–30,000 eggs per day. The life cycle is soil-based: eggs passed in feces hatch in warm, moist soil within 1–2 days, develop through two larval stages into infective filariform (L3) larvae within 5–10 days, and penetrate human skin — usually the soles of bare feet. A. duodenale can also infect via the oral and transmammary routes, unlike Necator americanus; infective larvae may even arrest in host tissues (hypobiosis) and resume development months later. After skin penetration, L3 larvae enter the bloodstream, travel to the lungs, are coughed up and swallowed, then mature in the small intestine. Prepatent period is 4–8 weeks. The principal morbidity is iron-deficiency anemia from chronic blood loss: a single A. duodenale worm removes approximately 0.2 mL of blood per day — roughly three times the blood loss of Necator americanus — making A. duodenale the more pathogenic of the two species per worm. Ancylostomiasis is a neglected tropical disease (NTD) targeted by WHO mass drug administration (MDA) programs using mebendazole and albendazole. It falls under the care of infectious disease specialists and, when complications arise, also involves gastroenterology and hematology.

typesClassification.description: An intensely itchy, erythematous, papular or vesicular skin rash occurring at the larval entry site — typically the foot or ankle — within hours to 2 days of skin penetration.

Occurs when A. duodenale larvae are ingested (e.g., via contaminated vegetables); symptoms include nausea, vomiting, pharyngeal irritation, and cough, reflecting larval migration via the esophagus and upper GI tract. Unique to A. duodenale.
As larvae pass through pulmonary capillaries and alveoli, transient cough, wheezing, and mild eosinophilic pneumonia (Löffler-like syndrome) may occur, usually lasting 1–2 weeks.
Adult worms in the small intestine produce progressive iron-deficiency anemia, hypoalbuminemia, and GI symptoms; this is the dominant clinical phase and the cause of most morbidity.

primarySymptoms: Itchy, red, raised papules or blisters at the skin entry site (ground itch), predominantly on the feet and lower legs, appearing within hours to days of barefoot contact with contaminated soil.
Chronic iron-deficiency anemia causing fatigue, pallor, dizziness, exertional dyspnea, and palpitations — the predominant morbidity of established intestinal infection.
Epigastric or periumbilical abdominal pain, often described as gnawing or colicky, from worm attachment and intestinal wall irritation.
Nausea, anorexia, and intermittent diarrhea alternating with normal stools during intestinal infection.
Peripheral edema of the feet and lower legs from hypoalbuminemia and protein malnutrition in heavy, chronic infections.
Cognitive impairment and reduced school performance in children with heavy worm burdens, attributed to anemia and micronutrient deficiency.
Growth faltering and underweight in children chronically infected in endemic settings, reflecting the combined burden of anemia and protein loss.
Pica (craving for non-food substances such as clay or soil), which is a recognized feature of hookworm-associated iron deficiency in some populations.
Cough and wheezing during the pulmonary larval migration phase, usually mild and self-limiting.
High-output cardiac signs (tachycardia, flow murmur, cardiomegaly) in severe anemia from heavy chronic infection.
earlyWarningSigns: Localized itching and a small raised rash on the foot or lower leg after walking barefoot on soil in an endemic area.
Mild cough and shortness of breath appearing 1–2 weeks after probable larval exposure.
Unexplained fatigue and pallor in a child or adult living in or returning from a tropical region with known soil contamination.
Anorexia and vague abdominal discomfort without another identifiable cause in a traveler or migrant from an endemic region.
emergencySigns: Severe anemia with hemoglobin below 7 g/dL causing marked fatigue, chest pain, or heart failure signs.
Significant gastrointestinal bleeding evidenced by tarry stools (melena) from heavy worm attachment.
Acute respiratory distress during the pulmonary migration phase, particularly in children or immunocompromised individuals.
Signs of heart failure — severe bilateral edema, orthopnea, or crepitations — from high-output cardiac stress caused by profound anemia.
Neurological symptoms such as confusion or syncope from critically low hemoglobin, requiring urgent blood transfusion assessment.

causes.cause: Skin penetration by infective L3 larvae in contaminated soil
causes.description: The predominant route of infection: filariform larvae in warm, moist fecally contaminated soil penetrate unprotected skin — especially the interdigital spaces and soles of bare feet. The larvae actively migrate using thermotropic and chemotropic cues. Transmission requires soil temperatures above 15°C and sustained moisture, conditions typical of tropical and subtropical agricultural zones.

causes.cause: Oral ingestion of infective larvae (A. duodenale specific)
causes.description: Ancylostoma duodenale, unlike Necator americanus, can infect via the oral route when contaminated soil, vegetables, or water containing L3 larvae is ingested. This produces Wakana disease with pharyngeal and upper GI symptoms and explains transmission in communities where soil-contaminated raw vegetables are consumed.

causes.cause: Contamination of agricultural soil with human feces
causes.description: Open defecation and the use of untreated human waste (night soil) as fertilizer maintain high larval densities in the top 1–2 cm of warm soil. Farmers, construction workers, and children who play barefoot are exposed through direct soil contact.

Communities without toilets or latrines discharge hookworm eggs into the peridomestic environment, sustaining the transmission cycle generation to generation. Lack of clean footwear amplifies skin-contact transmission.

causes.cause: Transmammary transmission to infants (A. duodenale specific)
causes.description: Hypobiotic (arrested) larvae in the mother's tissues can reactivate during lactation and be passed to nursing infants through breast milk, producing severe anemia in infants under 6 months — a phenomenon documented primarily with A. duodenale in Africa and Asia.

riskFactors.description: Direct skin-soil contact is the primary transmission route. Wearing shoes or sandals in hookworm-endemic areas virtually eliminates transmission via the cutaneous route.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in tropical or subtropical low-income countries
riskFactors.description: Warm, humid climate sustains larval development in soil, and poor sanitation maintains fecal-soil contamination; sub-Saharan Africa, South and Southeast Asia, and parts of Latin America bear the highest burden.
Farmers, tea plantation workers, and vegetable growers spend prolonged time in direct soil contact; hookworm prevalence among agricultural workers in endemic areas can reach 80%.

Children accumulate the heaviest worm burdens because of barefoot play in soil and developing immunity; nutritional consequences — growth stunting and cognitive impairment — are most severe at this age.

Pregnant women with hookworm infection are at heightened risk of severe anemia and iron deficiency because fetal demands compound the blood-loss anemia; WHO recommends routine deworming during pregnancy in endemic countries.
Pre-existing nutritional deficiency amplifies the clinical impact of even moderate worm burdens; repletion of iron is an integral part of treatment alongside anthelmintics.
HIV infection, malnutrition, and corticosteroid use can impair the immune response that limits worm burden, allowing heavier infections with more severe anemia and protein loss.

affectedDemographics: School-age children (5–14 years) in tropical and subtropical regions — the group most targeted by WHO mass deworming programs
Pregnant women in endemic countries, in whom hookworm anemia poses maternal and fetal risk
Agricultural and manual workers with sustained barefoot soil contact in endemic areas
Infants in areas where A. duodenale is prevalent, at risk of transmammary infection and severe neonatal anemia
Travelers and migrants who have spent time in endemic rural settings without footwear
Indigenous and rural communities in parts of Australia, sub-Saharan Africa, India, and Southeast Asia
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of ancylostomiasis is established by identifying hookworm eggs in stool. The Kato-Katz thick-smear technique is the WHO-recommended method for routine surveys and clinical diagnosis in endemic settings; it quantifies egg output in eggs per gram (epg) of feces, which correlates with worm burden: less than 2,000 epg is light, 2,000–3,999 is moderate, and ≥ 4,000 is heavy infection. Single stool examination has sensitivity around 60–70%; three specimens taken on separate days improves sensitivity to above 90%. Hookworm eggs are oval, thin-shelled, and measure 55–75 × 35–40 μm; species identification based on eggs alone is not possible — A. duodenale and N. americanus require larval culture (Harada-Mori technique) or PCR for differentiation, which is important in epidemiological but rarely clinical contexts. In returned travelers or migrants, direct smear, formalin-ethyl acetate sedimentation, and agar plate culture are alternatives. Peripheral blood eosinophilia is common during larval migration and early intestinal infection but may be absent in chronic infection. A full blood count confirming microcytic hypochromic anemia, low serum ferritin, and reduced transferrin saturation establishes the degree of iron deficiency and guides iron supplementation. Stool occult blood is frequently positive in moderate-to-heavy infections. The main differential diagnoses include other intestinal nematodes (Ascaris, Trichuris), iron deficiency from dietary causes, peptic ulcer disease, and inflammatory bowel disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The WHO-recommended quantitative technique that identifies hookworm eggs per gram of feces, distinguishing light (< 2,000 epg), moderate, and heavy infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You provide a fresh stool sample in a clean container; a thick smear is prepared on a glass slide, stained, and examined under a microscope. Results are usually available the same day in reference labs.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A rapid, simple method to detect hookworm eggs at the point of care; less sensitive than Kato-Katz but useful when specialized equipment is unavailable.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small amount of stool is placed on a glass slide with a saline drop and examined under a microscope within 30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Concentrates parasite eggs and larvae from stool, increasing sensitivity for light infections common in travelers returning from endemic regions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A preserved stool sample is processed in the laboratory; results take 1–2 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the anemia and eosinophilia associated with hookworm infection; microcytic, hypochromic anemia reflects chronic blood loss; eosinophilia is prominent during larval migration.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw; results within a few hours. Hemoglobin below 11 g/dL in women and 12 g/dL in men indicates clinically significant anemia.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms iron-deficiency status and guides the dose and duration of iron supplementation needed alongside anthelmintic treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood test; low ferritin (< 12 μg/L) is the most sensitive marker of iron depletion.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates Ancylostoma duodenale from Necator americanus by culturing stool to develop third-stage larvae that can be morphologically distinguished; primarily used in research and epidemiological studies.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Stool is cultured on filter paper for 7–10 days at 25°C; not routinely done in clinical management.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of ancylostomiasis combines anthelminthic therapy to eliminate intestinal worms with iron and protein repletion to reverse anemia and malnutrition. Albendazole 400 mg as a single oral dose is the first-line agent in most guidelines and WHO programs; cure rates for A. duodenale are approximately 72–95% with a single dose, rising to greater than 95% with a three-day course. Mebendazole 500 mg as a single dose is an alternative first-line agent, though its bioavailability is lower and single-dose cure rates for hookworm are somewhat inferior to albendazole. For heavy infections or in pregnant women in the second and third trimesters, a three-day course of albendazole 400 mg daily is preferred to maximize egg reduction. Iron supplementation — typically ferrous sulfate 60 mg elemental iron two to three times daily — is essential alongside anthelmintic therapy to replenish depleted stores; the duration depends on the severity of anemia and is usually 3–6 months. Protein and caloric supplementation address hypoalbuminemia in severe chronic infections. Severely anemic patients (hemoglobin < 7 g/dL) may require blood transfusion before or alongside anthelmintic therapy. Follow-up stool examination 2–4 weeks after treatment confirms parasitological cure; reinfection in endemic settings is common, and WHO MDA programs administer annual or biannual doses to school-age children and pregnant women to control the disease burden at the population level. In the first trimester of pregnancy, treatment is typically deferred to the second trimester because benzimidazole teratogenicity data in early pregnancy are limited.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole
medicalTreatments.description: A benzimidazole anthelmintic given as a single 400 mg oral dose; the most widely used first-line agent for ancylostomiasis in WHO-endorsed mass drug administration and individual treatment programs. For heavy infections or in pregnancy (second-third trimester), a three-day course (400 mg daily for 3 days) is preferred. Taken with or after food to improve absorption.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Single-dose cure rates of approximately 72–95% for A. duodenale; egg reduction rates consistently above 90% in clinical trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Mebendazole
medicalTreatments.description: A benzimidazole alternative given as a single 500 mg dose or 100 mg twice daily for 3 days. Also available in chewable formulations for children. Less bioavailable than albendazole, resulting in somewhat lower hookworm cure rates with single-dose therapy, but comparable with 3-day courses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Single-dose cure rates for hookworm typically 15–79% varying by study; 3-day regimen achieves 70–90% cure rates in most trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate or ferrous fumarate)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral iron is co-prescribed to treat and prevent hookworm-associated iron-deficiency anemia; ferrous sulfate 200 mg (60 mg elemental iron) two to three times daily. Duration is tailored to ferritin levels and hemoglobin response, typically 3–6 months. Vitamin C co-administration enhances absorption.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores hemoglobin by approximately 1–2 g/dL per month in compliant patients with iron-deficiency anemia; required alongside anthelmintics for full clinical recovery.

medicalTreatments.name: Blood transfusion
medicalTreatments.description: Packed red blood cells are indicated for severe symptomatic anemia (hemoglobin < 7 g/dL with hemodynamic compromise, or < 8–9 g/dL in pregnancy) that cannot be corrected quickly enough with oral iron alone. Given in conjunction with anthelmintic therapy, not as a substitute.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapidly corrects hemoglobin and resolves anemia-related cardiac stress; 1 unit of packed red cells raises hemoglobin approximately 1 g/dL in an adult.

alternativeTreatments.name: Improved sanitation and shoe-wearing (primary prevention)
alternativeTreatments.description: Providing and consistently using latrines, combined with wearing shoes, reduces transmission to near zero in previously endemic communities; these structural interventions are the only long-term solution to hookworm elimination.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary iron-rich food intake
alternativeTreatments.description: Consuming iron-rich foods (meat, legumes, leafy green vegetables) and vitamin C-containing foods that enhance non-heme iron absorption can partially offset the anemia of light hookworm infection, particularly in children who cannot immediately access anthelminthics.

whySeeSpecialist: Most cases of ancylostomiasis can be managed in primary care or by general practitioners, but an infectious disease specialist should be involved for heavy infections with severe anemia (hemoglobin < 7 g/dL), treatment failure after two courses of anthelminthics, suspected A. duodenale infection in pregnancy with early gestational age, or in immunocompromised patients. Hematology consultation is warranted for severe iron-deficiency anemia requiring parenteral iron or transfusion. Travelers returning with confirmed hookworm infection should be evaluated by a travel medicine or tropical disease specialist who can confirm species and assess for co-infections.
doctorSelectionGuide: In endemic regions, primary health care workers trained in WHO-endorsed deworming protocols can manage uncomplicated cases. In non-endemic high-income settings, a travel medicine physician, infectious disease specialist, or tropical medicine clinic is the best resource. Look for access to parasitology laboratory services capable of Kato-Katz stool analysis and, ideally, larval culture or PCR for species confirmation. For pregnant women with ancylostomiasis, a maternal-fetal medicine specialist should coordinate care alongside infectious disease.
preventionStrategies: Wear shoes or sandals at all times in areas known to have hookworm-contaminated soil, particularly in tropical rural or agricultural settings.
Use latrines and practice open-defecation-free sanitation to prevent soil contamination with hookworm eggs.
Wash hands thoroughly with soap after soil contact and before eating; wash vegetables grown in soil with clean water.
WHO recommends regular anthelminthic treatment (mass drug administration with albendazole or mebendazole) for school-age children, preschool children, and pregnant women in endemic countries.
Supplement iron intake in populations in endemic areas, especially children and pregnant women.
Avoid using untreated human feces as agricultural fertilizer; where night soil is used, composting to adequate temperatures can reduce larval viability.
lifestyleModifications: Wear protective footwear consistently when outdoors in tropical environments, especially after rain when larvae migrate to the surface.
Complete the full prescribed course of iron supplementation even after anthelmintic treatment, as anemia takes weeks to months to fully correct.
Maintain adequate dietary protein and iron intake to support recovery from hookworm-associated malnutrition.
Re-test stool 2–4 weeks after treatment to confirm egg clearance; retreat if still positive.
In endemic areas, accept periodic preventive deworming as part of community or school-based programs.
Families with young children should prioritize latrine use and shoe-wearing for children to reduce the heaviest-burden age group's exposure.

recommended: Iron-rich foods: red meat, poultry, legumes (lentils, kidney beans), dark leafy greens (spinach, kale)
Vitamin C-containing foods (citrus, bell peppers, tomatoes) eaten with iron-rich plant foods to enhance non-heme iron absorption
Protein-rich foods to address hypoalbuminemia in chronic heavy infections
Fortified cereals and grains where available as a cost-effective iron source in low-income settings
avoid: Tea, coffee, and calcium-rich foods consumed at the same time as iron supplements or iron-rich meals, as they inhibit iron absorption
Raw or unwashed vegetables grown in hookworm-endemic soil
Diets high in phytates (unfermented whole grains, raw legumes) without vitamin C, which reduce iron bioavailability

exerciseGuidelines: During active heavy infection with significant anemia, strenuous physical activity should be restricted as the cardiovascular system may not tolerate the oxygen demand. Gentle daily movement is acceptable for mild infections. As hemoglobin rises with iron therapy (typically over 4–8 weeks), activity can be progressively restored. Children who have been treated and are recovering should be encouraged to return to normal play and school physical activity once hemoglobin normalizes.
prognosis: The prognosis of ancylostomiasis is excellent with timely treatment. A single dose of albendazole or mebendazole eliminates the majority of worms, and iron supplementation corrects anemia over 2–6 months. In children, recovery from anemia is associated with significant improvements in physical growth, cognitive performance, and school attendance, with studies documenting measurable gains in height-for-age and cognitive test scores within 12 months of treatment. Reinfection in endemic settings is nearly universal without behavioral and environmental changes; this is why WHO MDA programs administer annual preventive doses rather than relying on single treatment. Untreated heavy infection can contribute to significant morbidity — stunting, intellectual disability, and maternal and infant mortality — but these outcomes are preventable. Case fatality from ancylostomiasis alone is very low, occurring mainly in neonates with transmammary infection or in severely malnourished patients with concomitant infections.
recoveryTimeline: Gastrointestinal symptoms typically resolve within 1–2 weeks of successful anthelmintic treatment. Blood eosinophilia begins declining within 2–4 weeks. Hemoglobin recovery with iron supplementation proceeds at roughly 1–2 g/dL per month, so moderate anemia (Hb 8–10 g/dL) requires approximately 2–3 months of iron therapy. Ferritin and iron stores take 3–6 months to fully replenish. In children with growth faltering, measurable catch-up growth may take 6–12 months. Reinfection can occur within weeks to months in high-transmission settings.
complications: Severe iron-deficiency anemia — hemoglobin can fall below 5–6 g/dL in heavy untreated infection, causing high-output heart failure, developmental impairment in children, and maternal and fetal risk in pregnancy.
Hypoalbuminemia and protein-energy malnutrition — chronic blood and protein loss in heavy infection leads to peripheral edema, poor wound healing, and immune suppression.
Intrauterine growth restriction and low birth weight — hookworm anemia in pregnancy reduces fetal oxygen and nutrient delivery, contributing to preterm birth and neonatal mortality.

Cognitive impairment and stunted development — iron-deficiency anemia in early childhood impairs myelination and neurodevelopment, with long-term effects on school performance measurable even after treatment.

Löffler syndrome (eosinophilic pneumonia) — during larval lung migration, transient pulmonary infiltrates with cough and wheeze; self-limiting but may require short-course corticosteroids in severe cases.
Secondary infections — the immune impairment of severe malnutrition increases susceptibility to respiratory, gastrointestinal, and other infections in heavily infected individuals.

supportResources.name: WHO — Soil-transmitted helminth infections
supportResources.description: WHO fact sheet on hookworm, Ascaris, and Trichuris infections, with global prevalence data and mass drug administration program information.

supportResources.name: CDC — Hookworm
supportResources.description: Patient and clinician resource on hookworm biology, diagnosis, treatment, and prevention from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Parasites and Foodborne Illnesses
supportResources.description: Plain-English NIH resource on parasitic GI infections and when to seek medical care.

sources.title: WHO — Soil-transmitted helminth infections fact sheet, 2023

confusedWithConditions.name: Necatoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Necator americanus infection produces very similar intestinal and anemia symptoms but is endemic mainly in the Americas and sub-Saharan Africa, causes less blood loss per worm, and cannot infect via the oral or transmammary route; distinguishing the two requires larval culture or PCR.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ascariasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ascariasis also causes eosinophilia and pulmonary migration but produces large roundworms detectable in stool by the naked eye and does not cause the hookworm-typical iron-deficiency anemia at low worm burdens.

confusedWithConditions.name: Strongyloidiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Strongyloides stercoralis also penetrates skin and produces eosinophilia and GI symptoms, but can autoinfect and persist for decades, cause hyperinfection syndrome in immunocompromised hosts, and its larvae (not just eggs) are found in stool.

confusedWithConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Schistosoma species also penetrate skin in water (not soil) and cause eosinophilia and chronic organ damage, but target the liver, bladder, and vasculature rather than the intestinal mucosa, and are distinguished by schistosome eggs in stool or urine.

relatedConditions.name: Necatoriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: The closely related hookworm species Necator americanus causes identical clinical disease and is distinguished only by geography, worm morphology, and blood-loss rate per worm.

relatedConditions.name: Trichuriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Whipworm infection frequently co-occurs with hookworm in children in endemic settings; both are soil-transmitted nematodes targeted by the same WHO MDA programs.

relatedConditions.name: Ascariasis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most prevalent soil-transmitted helminthiasis; co-infection with hookworm in the same child is the norm in high-burden settings, and both are treated by the same anthelminthics.

relatedConditions.name: Strongyloidiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another skin-penetrating nematode with a similar epidemiological profile; both infections are targeted by deworming programs and present with eosinophilia.

relatedConditions.name: Helminthiasis (unspecified)
relatedConditions.relevance: The broad category encompassing multiple concurrent worm infections, which is common in hookworm-endemic communities where polyparasitism is the rule.

faqs.question: What is ancylostomiasis and how do you get it?
faqs.answer: Ancylostomiasis is a hookworm infection caused by Ancylostoma duodenale. The worm's larvae live in warm, moist soil contaminated by human feces. They penetrate bare skin — usually the feet — travel through the bloodstream to the lungs, are swallowed, and mature in the small intestine, where they attach and feed on blood.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of hookworm infection?
faqs.answer: The most important symptom is chronic iron-deficiency anemia causing fatigue, pallor, dizziness, and shortness of breath from blood loss by the worms. Earlier symptoms include itchy skin rash at the entry site, mild cough during larval lung migration, and abdominal discomfort. Heavy infections in children cause growth delay and reduced cognitive performance.

faqs.question: How is hookworm infection diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made by examining a stool sample under a microscope to find hookworm eggs. The Kato-Katz thick-smear technique is standard and quantifies the number of eggs per gram of stool. Blood tests showing eosinophilia, low hemoglobin, and low iron (ferritin) support the diagnosis.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for ancylostomiasis?
faqs.answer: A single oral dose of albendazole 400 mg is the first-line treatment, with cure rates of 72–95% for A. duodenale. Mebendazole 500 mg single dose is an alternative. Iron supplementation — usually 60 mg elemental iron two to three times daily for 3–6 months — is equally important to correct the anemia caused by blood loss.

faqs.question: Can hookworm cause anemia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hookworm anemia is one of the leading causes of iron-deficiency anemia in tropical regions. Each worm removes approximately 0.2 mL of blood per day; a heavy infection with hundreds of worms can cause hemoglobin to fall well below safe levels. Treatment with both anthelmintics and iron supplements is required.

faqs.question: Is ancylostomiasis contagious between people?
faqs.answer: Ancylostomiasis is not directly transmitted from person to person. It requires soil as an intermediate environment — eggs passed in feces develop into infective larvae in warm moist soil. A person with the infection cannot pass it directly to others through casual contact.

faqs.question: How do I prevent hookworm infection?
faqs.answer: Wear shoes at all times when outdoors in tropical or subtropical regions, especially in soil that may be contaminated. Using latrines prevents soil contamination. Washing vegetables and hands thoroughly also reduces risk. In endemic countries, WHO-recommended deworming programs offer periodic anthelminthic treatment to children and pregnant women.

faqs.question: What is ground itch in hookworm infection?
faqs.answer: Ground itch is the local skin reaction that occurs when hookworm larvae penetrate the skin, usually the foot. It causes intense itching with a red, raised rash or blisters at the entry site within hours to days of exposure. The rash settles on its own within 1–2 weeks as larvae move deeper into the body.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from hookworm infection?
faqs.answer: Intestinal worms are eliminated within 1–2 weeks of anthelmintic treatment. However, recovery from anemia takes much longer — typically 2–6 months of iron supplementation to normalize hemoglobin and iron stores. In children with growth delays, catch-up growth may continue for 6–12 months.

faqs.question: Can ancylostomiasis affect pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hookworm anemia during pregnancy increases the risk of maternal iron deficiency, intrauterine growth restriction, low birth weight, and preterm delivery. WHO recommends anthelminthic treatment (albendazole or mebendazole) in the second and third trimesters of pregnancy in endemic countries, combined with iron and folate supplementation.

faqs.question: What blood test results suggest hookworm?
faqs.answer: Key blood findings include raised eosinophils (most pronounced during larval migration), microcytic hypochromic anemia, low serum ferritin, reduced transferrin saturation, and low total iron-binding capacity. Stool examination, not blood tests alone, confirms the diagnosis.

faqs.question: How is Ancylostoma different from Necator?
faqs.answer: Both are hookworms causing very similar disease, but Ancylostoma duodenale removes about three times more blood per worm than Necator americanus and can also infect via swallowing and breast milk. A. duodenale predominates in the Mediterranean, Middle East, and Asia; N. americanus in the Americas and Africa. Eggs look identical; species separation needs larval culture or PCR.

faqs.question: Can children get hookworm from playing in soil?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children who play barefoot in soil contaminated with human feces in tropical regions are at high risk of hookworm infection. School-age children often have the heaviest worm burdens. This is why WHO mass deworming programs specifically target preschool and school-age children in endemic areas.

faqs.question: Does hookworm affect the lungs?
faqs.answer: During the larval migration phase, larvae pass through the pulmonary capillaries and alveoli, causing a transient cough and mild wheeze that usually lasts 1–2 weeks. This Löffler-like eosinophilic pneumonia is self-limiting in most cases. Severe pulmonary symptoms are uncommon and usually resolve once larvae leave the lungs.

faqs.question: Will I need to be treated again after hookworm treatment?
faqs.answer: In endemic settings, reinfection after treatment is common because larvae remain in the soil environment. WHO recommends annual or biannual preventive deworming in high-risk populations. In non-endemic settings or after returning from travel, a follow-up stool test 2–4 weeks after treatment confirms cure, and retreatment is only needed if still positive.

faqs.question: Is hookworm visible in the stool?
faqs.answer: Adult hookworms are tiny (8–13 mm) and rarely visible to the naked eye in stool. Diagnosis requires laboratory examination of a stool sample under a microscope to identify the microscopic eggs. Unlike Ascaris, hookworm adults are not typically passed in the stool and are not visible on routine inspection.

faqs.question: What happens if hookworm is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated hookworm infection leads to progressive iron-deficiency anemia that worsens over months to years. In children, it causes stunted growth and impaired cognitive development. In pregnant women, it increases the risk of maternal mortality and adverse birth outcomes. In severe cases, the anemia can cause heart failure.

faqs.question: Can I get hookworm from pets?
faqs.answer: Dog and cat hookworms (A. caninum, A. braziliense) can penetrate human skin and cause a creeping itchy rash called cutaneous larva migrans, but these species do not typically mature to adult worms in humans. Human hookworm (A. duodenale) comes from soil contaminated with human feces, not directly from household pets.

faqs.question: What specialist treats hookworm infection?
faqs.answer: Uncomplicated hookworm is managed by a general practitioner or primary care clinician. An infectious disease specialist or tropical medicine physician is consulted for severe anemia, treatment failure, pregnancy complications, or in travelers from non-endemic countries. A hematologist may be involved for severe iron-deficiency anemia requiring parenteral iron.

metaDescription: Ancylostomiasis is a hookworm infection causing iron-deficiency anemia, fatigue and skin rash. Learn causes, diagnosis and treatment with albendazole.
